tasks.title: Project Foundation and Service Integration
tasks.description: Initialize the Next.js project with Tailwind CSS. Set up environment variables and install necessary SDKs for Clerk, Supabase, and Sanity. Establish basic connections to all three services.
tasks.details: Run `npx create-next-app@latest --typescript --tailwind --eslint`. Install `@clerk/nextjs`, `@supabase/supabase-js`, and `@sanity/client`. Create a `.env.local` file and populate it with API keys and project URLs for Clerk, Supabase, and Sanity. Create a basic Vercel project and link it to the repository for CI/CD.
tasks.testStrategy: Verify that the Next.js application runs locally without errors. Confirm that basic client instances for Supabase and Sanity can be initialized using the environment variables. Check that the Clerk provider can be wrapped around the root layout.

subtasks.title: Initialize Next.js Project with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS
subtasks.description: Create the foundational Next.js application using the specified command-line tool, including configurations for TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and ESLint.
subtasks.details: Execute the command `npx create-next-app@latest --typescript --tailwind --eslint` in the terminal to scaffold the initial project structure and files.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify that the new Next.js application can be started locally using `npm run dev` and is accessible without errors in a web browser.

subtasks.title: Install Service SDKs for Clerk, Supabase, and Sanity
subtasks.description: Add the necessary client libraries for Clerk, Supabase, and Sanity as dependencies to the newly created Next.js project.
subtasks.details: Run the command `npm install @clerk/nextjs @supabase/supabase-js @sanity/client` to install the required packages.
subtasks.testStrategy: Check the `package.json` file to confirm that `@clerk/nextjs`, `@supabase/supabase-js`, and `@sanity/client` are listed under dependencies.

subtasks.title: Configure Environment Variables for Services
subtasks.description: Create a local environment file and populate it with the necessary API keys and project URLs for Clerk, Supabase, and Sanity.
subtasks.details: Create a `.env.local` file in the project root. Obtain the required credentials (e.g., `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY`, `CLERK_SECRET_KEY`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY`, `SANITY_PROJECT_ID`) from the respective service dashboards and add them to the file.
subtasks.testStrategy: Create a temporary test page to log `process.env` variables and confirm they are loaded correctly within the application without exposing them on the client-side unless prefixed with `NEXT_PUBLIC_`.

subtasks.title: Implement Basic Service Client Initializations
subtasks.description: Create utility files to initialize the clients for Supabase and Sanity, and wrap the application's root layout with the Clerk provider to enable authentication context.
subtasks.details: Create a `lib/supabaseClient.ts` file to initialize and export the Supabase client. Create a `lib/sanityClient.ts` for the Sanity client. In `app/layout.tsx`, import and wrap the root component with `<ClerkProvider>`.
subtasks.testStrategy: Confirm that the application compiles and runs without errors. Verify that basic, unauthenticated test queries against Supabase and Sanity can be made successfully. Check that wrapping the layout with ClerkProvider does not break the application.

subtasks.title: Create and Link Vercel Project for CI/CD
subtasks.description: Set up a new project on Vercel and connect it to the source code repository to establish a continuous integration and deployment pipeline.
subtasks.details: Create a new project in the Vercel dashboard. Link it to the GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket repository containing the Next.js application. Ensure the framework preset is correctly identified as Next.js.
subtasks.testStrategy: Push a commit to the main branch of the repository and verify that a new deployment is automatically triggered on Vercel. Check the Vercel deployment logs for a successful build and ensure the deployed site is accessible via its Vercel URL.

tasks.title: Sanity CMS Schema Definition and Studio Deployment
tasks.description: Define and configure the content schemas in the Sanity project as specified in the PRD. Deploy the Sanity Studio to Vercel so that administrative staff can begin content creation immediately.
tasks.details: In the Sanity project, define the schemas for `post`, `guide`, and `page`. The `guide` schema must include fields for `title`, `slug`, `summary`, `body` (rich text), `ageCategory` (string options: U10, U12, etc.), and `targetRole` (string options: 'coach', 'staff'). Deploy the Sanity Studio as a separate application or within a route in the Next.js app (e.g., `/studio`).
tasks.testStrategy: Access the deployed Sanity Studio URL. Verify that admins can log in and create new documents for each schema type (`post`, `guide`, `page`). Confirm that all fields, especially `ageCategory` and `targetRole` on the `guide` schema, are present and functional.

subtasks.title: Initialize Sanity Studio Project
subtasks.description: Set up the Sanity Studio project structure, either as a standalone project or embedded within the Next.js application, and connect it to the Sanity cloud project.
subtasks.details: Run `npx sanity init` to create the project files. Configure the `sanity.config.ts` file with the correct `projectId` and `dataset` from the Sanity manage dashboard. Ensure the project is linked to the correct Sanity account.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run `npm run dev` or `sanity start` in the studio directory and verify that the Sanity Studio loads locally in the browser without errors.

subtasks.title: Define 'guide' Content Schema
subtasks.description: Create the detailed schema definition for the 'guide' document type, including all specified fields and validation rules.
subtasks.details: Create a `schemas/guide.ts` file. Define fields for `title` (string), `slug` (slug type with title as source), `summary` (text), `body` (block content/rich text), `ageCategory` (string with a predefined list of options like 'U10', 'U12'), and `targetRole` (string with options 'coach', 'staff').
subtasks.testStrategy: In the local studio, attempt to create a new 'guide' document. Verify that all fields are present, the dropdown options for `ageCategory` and `targetRole` are correct, and the slug is auto-generated from the title.

subtasks.title: Define 'post' and 'page' Schemas
subtasks.description: Create the schema definition files for the standard 'post' and 'page' document types.
subtasks.details: Create `schemas/post.ts` and `schemas/page.ts`. For both, define standard fields like `title` (string), `slug` (slug type), and `body` (block content). The `post` schema can optionally include fields like `author` or `publishedAt`.
subtasks.testStrategy: In the local studio, verify that 'post' and 'page' appear as creatable document types. Create one of each and confirm their basic fields are functional.

subtasks.title: Register Schemas and Configure Studio Desk
subtasks.description: Integrate all defined schemas into the main Sanity configuration and organize the Studio's desk structure for better usability.
subtasks.details: In `sanity.config.ts`, import the `guide`, `post`, and `page` schema definitions and add them to the `schema.types` array. Use the `deskTool` plugin to customize the navigation pane, potentially grouping documents by their type.
subtasks.testStrategy: Load the local Sanity Studio and check the main navigation pane. Confirm that `Guide`, `Post`, and `Page` are listed as content types, matching the configured desk structure.

subtasks.title: Deploy Sanity Studio to Vercel
subtasks.description: Configure the project for deployment and deploy the Sanity Studio to a public URL on Vercel.
subtasks.details: Ensure the build command for the Sanity Studio is correctly configured in `package.json` and Vercel project settings. Add all necessary environment variables to Vercel (e.g., `SANITY_STUDIO_PROJECT_ID`, `SANITY_STUDIO_DATASET`). If embedding in Next.js, ensure the `/studio` route is correctly configured. Trigger a deployment.
subtasks.testStrategy: Access the Vercel deployment URL for the studio. Log in with a Sanity account. Verify that the studio is fully functional and that new content for all schema types can be created and published.

tasks.title: Supabase Database Schema Setup
tasks.description: Set up the required tables in Supabase to store application-specific user data that complements Clerk's authentication data. This includes tables for users and clubs.
tasks.details: Using the Supabase dashboard or SQL scripts, create the `clubs` table with `id` (UUID, primary key), `name` (text), and `location` (text). Create the `users` table with `id` (UUID, primary key, default `uuid_generate_v4()`), `clerk_id` (text, unique), `role` (text, e.g., 'admin', 'coach', 'staff'), and `club_id` (UUID, foreign key referencing `clubs.id`).
tasks.testStrategy: Verify in the Supabase dashboard that both `clubs` and `users` tables exist with the correct columns, types, and constraints. Manually insert a sample club and a sample user to ensure data integrity and foreign key relationships work as expected.

subtasks.title: Enable UUID Extension in Supabase
subtasks.description: Enable the `uuid-ossp` extension within the Supabase project to support the automatic generation of UUIDs for primary keys.
subtasks.details: Navigate to the 'Database' -> 'Extensions' section in the Supabase dashboard. Find `uuid-ossp` in the list and enable it. This is a prerequisite for setting a default UUID value on the `users` table's primary key.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify that `uuid-ossp` is listed as enabled in the 'Extensions' section of the Supabase dashboard.

subtasks.title: Create the 'clubs' Table
subtasks.description: Create the `clubs` table to store information about each club in the application.
subtasks.details: Using the Supabase Table Editor or a SQL script, create the `clubs` table. Define the columns: `id` as a UUID and primary key, `name` as text, and `location` as text.
subtasks.testStrategy: Confirm in the Supabase Table Editor that the `clubs` table exists and contains the `id`, `name`, and `location` columns with the correct data types and `id` set as the primary key.

subtasks.title: Create the 'users' Table
subtasks.description: Create the `users` table to store application-specific user data that complements the authentication data from Clerk.
subtasks.details: Using the Supabase Table Editor or a SQL script, create the `users` table. Define the columns: `id` as a UUID, primary key with a default value of `uuid_generate_v4()`, `clerk_id` as text, `role` as text, and `club_id` as a UUID.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify in the Supabase Table Editor that the `users` table exists with all specified columns, data types, and that the `id` column has its default value function correctly configured.

subtasks.title: Establish 'users' Table Constraints and Foreign Key
subtasks.description: Apply the necessary constraints to the `users` table, including the foreign key relationship to the `clubs` table and the unique constraint on `clerk_id`.
subtasks.details: Add a foreign key constraint to the `users.club_id` column, making it reference `clubs.id`. Also, apply a unique constraint on the `users.clerk_id` column to ensure each Clerk user can only have one entry.
subtasks.testStrategy: Inspect the table relationships and constraints in the Supabase dashboard to confirm the foreign key is active. Attempt to add a user with a `club_id` that does not exist in the `clubs` table; the operation should fail.

subtasks.title: Seed and Verify Schema with Sample Data
subtasks.description: Insert sample data into both tables to perform a final validation of the schema, constraints, and relationships.
subtasks.details: First, manually insert a sample record into the `clubs` table. Note its `id`. Then, insert a sample record into the `users` table, using the previously created club's `id` for the `club_id` field and a test value for `clerk_id`.
subtasks.testStrategy: Confirm that both records can be inserted successfully. Verify the data integrity by checking the values in the dashboard. Attempt to insert a second user with the same `clerk_id` to ensure the unique constraint prevents it.

tasks.title: Implement User Authentication with Clerk
tasks.description: Integrate Clerk into the Next.js application to handle all user authentication flows, including sign-in, sign-up, and user profile management.
tasks.details: Wrap the root layout (`app/layout.tsx`) with `<ClerkProvider>`. Create sign-in (`/sign-in`) and sign-up (`/sign-up`) pages using Clerk's pre-built components. Implement protected routes using Clerk's middleware. Add the `<UserButton>` component to the header for logged-in users to manage their account and sign out.
tasks.testStrategy: Test the full authentication flow: a new user can sign up, sign out, and sign back in. Verify that navigating to a protected route redirects unauthenticated users to the sign-in page. Confirm the UserButton displays correctly and is functional.

subtasks.title: Install Clerk SDK and Configure Environment Variables
subtasks.description: Install the `@clerk/nextjs` package and set up the necessary environment variables for Clerk integration.
subtasks.details: Run `npm install @clerk/nextjs`. Obtain the `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY` and `CLERK_SECRET_KEY` from the Clerk project dashboard and add them to the `.env.local` file to authenticate with the Clerk API.

subtasks.title: Wrap Root Layout with ClerkProvider
subtasks.description: Integrate the Clerk context into the Next.js application by wrapping the root layout with the `<ClerkProvider>` component.
subtasks.details: Modify the `app/layout.tsx` file to import `ClerkProvider` from `@clerk/nextjs`. Wrap the main content of the `<body>` tag with `<ClerkProvider>` to make authentication state and hooks available globally.

subtasks.title: Create Sign-In and Sign-Up Pages
subtasks.description: Build the user-facing authentication pages using Clerk's pre-built UI components for sign-in and sign-up.
subtasks.details: Create two new route files: `app/sign-in/[[...sign-in]]/page.tsx` and `app/sign-up/[[...sign-up]]/page.tsx`. Import and render the `<SignIn />` and `<SignUp />` components from `@clerk/nextjs` within these pages to handle the respective authentication flows.

subtasks.title: Implement Middleware for Protected Routes
subtasks.description: Configure Clerk's middleware to protect application routes, ensuring only authenticated users can access certain pages.
subtasks.details: Create a `middleware.ts` file at the root of the project. Import and export `authMiddleware` from `@clerk/nextjs`. Configure the `publicRoutes` array to include pages like the homepage, sign-in, and sign-up, while all other routes will be protected by default.

subtasks.title: Add UserButton to Header for Account Management
subtasks.description: Integrate the `<UserButton>` component into the application's header to provide logged-in users with access to profile management and sign-out functionality.
subtasks.details: In the application's main header or navigation component, import and render the `<UserButton>` from `@clerk/nextjs`. Use Clerk's `<SignedIn>` component to ensure the button only appears for authenticated users. Configure the `afterSignOutUrl` prop to redirect users to the homepage after they sign out.

tasks.title: User Data Sync with Clerk Webhooks and Supabase
tasks.description: Create a mechanism to synchronize user data between Clerk and Supabase. When a new user signs up via Clerk, a corresponding entry should be created in the Supabase `users` table.
tasks.details: Use Clerk Webhooks to listen for `user.created` events. Create a new API route in Next.js (e.g., `/api/webhooks/clerk`) to handle incoming webhook requests. The handler will verify the webhook signature, then extract the `id` and other relevant data from the event payload. It will then use the Supabase client to `INSERT` a new row into the `users` table, mapping the Clerk `id` to the `clerk_id` field and assigning a default role (e.g., 'coach').
tasks.testStrategy: Configure a webhook in the Clerk dashboard pointing to the deployed API route. Create a new user through the sign-up flow. Verify that the webhook is triggered and that a corresponding user record appears in the Supabase `users` table with the correct `clerk_id` and a default role.

subtasks.title: Configure Clerk Webhook Endpoint
subtasks.description: Set up a new webhook endpoint in the Clerk dashboard to listen for the `user.created` event. This is the first step to enable communication from Clerk to the Next.js application.
subtasks.details: In the Clerk Dashboard, navigate to the Webhooks section. Create a new endpoint, providing the future URL of the API route (e.g., `https://<your-domain>/api/webhooks/clerk`). Select only the `user.created` event. Copy the generated signing secret and store it securely as an environment variable (`CLERK_WEBHOOK_SECRET`).
subtasks.testStrategy: Use the 'Send test webhook' feature in the Clerk dashboard to confirm the endpoint is reachable once the API route is deployed. The initial test will likely fail until the route is created, but it confirms the Clerk-side configuration.

subtasks.title: Create Next.js API Route for Webhook
subtasks.description: Implement the basic structure of the API route in the Next.js application that will receive and process incoming webhook requests from Clerk.
subtasks.details: Create a new file at `app/api/webhooks/clerk/route.ts`. Define an async `POST` function to handle the incoming requests. Ensure the route is configured to read the raw request body, as this is required for signature verification.
subtasks.testStrategy: Deploy the application with the basic API route. Trigger a test webhook from the Clerk dashboard and check the server logs to confirm the `POST` function is executed and receives a request.

subtasks.title: Implement Webhook Signature Verification
subtasks.description: Add security to the webhook handler by verifying the signature of incoming requests to ensure they are legitimate and originate from Clerk.
subtasks.details: Install the `svix` library. In the API route handler, use the `Webhook` class from `svix` to verify the request. Retrieve the `svix-id`, `svix-timestamp`, and `svix-signature` headers and the raw request body. Use the `CLERK_WEBHOOK_SECRET` environment variable to perform the verification. If verification fails, return a `400 Bad Request` response immediately.
subtasks.testStrategy: Trigger a valid test webhook from Clerk and confirm it passes verification. Use a tool like cURL or Postman to send a request with an invalid or missing signature and confirm the route returns a `400` error.

subtasks.title: Process Webhook Payload and Extract User Data
subtasks.description: After successful signature verification, parse the webhook event payload to extract the necessary user information for database insertion.
subtasks.details: Once the payload is verified, parse the JSON body. Check that the event `type` is `user.created`. From the `data` object within the payload, extract the user's `id`. This `id` will be used as the `clerk_id` in the Supabase table.
subtasks.testStrategy: Add logging within the API route to print the parsed event type and the extracted user ID. Trigger a `user.created` event by signing up a new user and check the server logs to confirm the correct data is extracted.

subtasks.title: Insert New User Record into Supabase
subtasks.description: Use the extracted user data from the webhook to create a corresponding new record in the Supabase `users` table.
subtasks.details: Initialize the Supabase client within the API route. Construct an object to be inserted, mapping the Clerk user `id` to the `clerk_id` field and setting the `role` field to the default value of 'coach'. Use the Supabase client's `.from('users').insert()` method to add the new row to the database. Include error handling for potential database issues.
subtasks.testStrategy: Create a new user via the Clerk sign-up flow. Verify that the webhook handler executes successfully and that a new corresponding record appears in the Supabase `users` table with the correct `clerk_id` and the 'coach' role.

tasks.title: Develop Public Information Portal
tasks.description: Build the public-facing pages of the website, such as News, 'Our Philosophy', and Tryout Info. All content for these pages will be fetched from the Sanity CMS.
tasks.details: Create Next.js pages using the App Router. For the News page, use the Sanity client to fetch all documents of type `post`. For static pages like 'Our Philosophy', fetch the corresponding `page` document. Use Portable Text components to render the rich text `body` field from Sanity. Style all pages with Tailwind CSS.
tasks.testStrategy: Create sample 'post' and 'page' documents in the Sanity Studio. Navigate to the corresponding pages in the Next.js app and verify that the content is fetched and rendered correctly, including titles, images, and rich text formatting.

subtasks.title: Implement Sanity Client and Data Fetching Functions
subtasks.description: Set up the Sanity client instance and create reusable server-side functions for fetching content. This includes creating a function to get all 'post' documents and another to get a single 'page' document by its slug.
subtasks.details: In a new `lib/sanity.ts` file, initialize the Sanity client using environment variables. Export two async functions: `getAllPosts()` which performs a GROQ query for `*[_type == "post"]`, and `getPageBySlug(slug)` which queries for `*[_type == "page" && slug.current == $slug][0]`. These functions will be used by the page components.
subtasks.testStrategy: Create test scripts or temporarily call these functions from a page's `console.log` to verify that they successfully connect to Sanity and return the expected array of posts and single page objects.

subtasks.title: Build the Main News Page (`/news`)
subtasks.description: Create the primary News page that lists all available news articles, serving as an index for all posts.
subtasks.details: Create a new page route at `/app/news/page.tsx`. Use the `getAllPosts()` function from the Sanity lib to fetch the data. Map over the returned posts to render a grid or list of article cards. Each card should display the post's title, publication date, and a link to the full article. Style the layout using Tailwind CSS.
subtasks.testStrategy: With sample 'post' documents in the CMS, navigate to the `/news` page in the local development environment. Verify that all posts are listed and that the links are correctly formatted (e.g., `/news/my-first-post`).

subtasks.title: Develop the Individual News Article Page (`/news/[slug]`)
subtasks.description: Build the dynamic page for displaying the full content of a single news article, including rendering the rich text body.
subtasks.details: Create a dynamic route at `/app/news/[slug]/page.tsx`. Fetch the specific post data using its slug. Install `@portabletext/react` and use the `<PortableText>` component to render the `body` field from the fetched post data. Display the post title and other metadata. This is the first implementation of the Portable Text renderer.
subtasks.testStrategy: Click on a link from the `/news` page. Verify the correct article's title and body are displayed. Check that basic rich text elements like paragraphs and headings are rendered.

subtasks.title: Create the Generic Static Page Template (`/[slug]`)
subtasks.description: Develop a reusable, dynamic page template to render static content like 'Our Philosophy' or 'Tryout Info' from 'page' documents in Sanity.
subtasks.details: Create a dynamic route at the root level, `/app/[slug]/page.tsx`. This page will use the `getPageBySlug()` function to fetch the corresponding 'page' document. It will then render the page's `title` and `body`, reusing the `<PortableText>` component implementation from the individual news article page.
subtasks.testStrategy: Create 'page' documents in Sanity with slugs like 'our-philosophy' and 'tryout-info'. Navigate to `/our-philosophy` and `/tryout-info` and verify that the correct content is fetched and rendered by the template.

subtasks.title: Style Portable Text Components with Tailwind CSS
subtasks.description: Define and apply consistent styling for all rich text elements rendered by the Portable Text component across all pages.
subtasks.details: Create a custom components object to pass to the `<PortableText>` renderer. In this object, define React components for various block types (`h1`, `h2`, `blockquote`, `ul`) and mark types (`link`). Apply specific Tailwind CSS classes within these components to ensure consistent typography and spacing for all rich text content.
subtasks.testStrategy: Create a test page or post in Sanity that includes all possible rich text elements (all heading levels, lists, blockquotes, links). View this page in the browser and confirm that every element is styled correctly and consistently according to the design specifications.

tasks.title: Create Secure Content Library with Role-Based Access
tasks.description: Implement the secure, login-only section of the site. The system will check the authenticated user's role from Supabase and display content from Sanity that is tagged for their specific role.
tasks.details: Create a protected route group for the secure portal (e.g., `/(portal)`). In a server component within this group, get the current user's `userId` from Clerk. Use this `userId` to query the Supabase `users` table to retrieve the user's `role`. Pass the role to child components. When fetching `guide` documents from Sanity, add a GROQ query filter to match the `targetRole` field with the user's role.
tasks.testStrategy: Log in as a user with the 'coach' role. Navigate to the secure portal and verify that only guides with `targetRole` set to 'coach' are visible. Log in as a user with a different role (e.g., 'staff') and confirm they see a different set of content. Unauthenticated users should be redirected.

subtasks.title: Implement Protected Route Group for Portal
subtasks.description: Create a Next.js route group, such as `/(portal)`, and configure Clerk's middleware to protect all routes within it. This ensures that only authenticated users can access the content library, redirecting any unauthenticated requests to the sign-in page.
subtasks.details: Modify the `middleware.ts` file to add `/(portal)(.*)` to the `protectedRoutes` array. Create a new directory structure `app/(portal)/` and add a basic `layout.tsx` and `page.tsx` file to establish the protected area.
subtasks.testStrategy: Attempt to navigate to a URL within the `/portal` path while not logged in. Verify that the application redirects to the `/sign-in` page. After logging in, confirm access to the portal page is granted.

subtasks.title: Fetch Authenticated User's Role from Supabase
subtasks.description: In a server component, preferably the layout for the `/(portal)` group, retrieve the current user's ID from Clerk. Use this ID to execute a query against the Supabase `users` table to fetch the user's assigned `role`.
subtasks.details: Use Clerk's `auth()` helper function to get the `userId`. Initialize the Supabase client and perform a `SELECT role FROM users WHERE clerk_id = $1` query, using the Clerk `userId` as the parameter. Handle the case where a user might not have a corresponding entry or role in Supabase.
subtasks.testStrategy: Log in as a user with a known role (e.g., 'coach') in the Supabase `users` table. Add a temporary `console.log` in the server component to verify that the correct role string is successfully fetched.

subtasks.title: Develop Sanity Fetch Function with Role-Based Filtering
subtasks.description: Create a reusable data-fetching function that queries Sanity for `guide` documents. This function must accept a user's role as an argument and use it within the GROQ query to filter guides where the `targetRole` field matches the provided role.
subtasks.details: Create a new function, for example `getGuidesByRole(role: string)`. The function will use the Sanity client to execute a GROQ query like `*[_type == "guide" && targetRole == $role]`. The `role` argument will be passed as a parameter to the query.
subtasks.testStrategy: Test the function in isolation by calling it directly with 'coach' and verifying it returns only guides with `targetRole` set to 'coach'. Repeat the test with a different role to ensure the filtering is dynamic and correct.

subtasks.title: Build UI to Display Filtered Content
subtasks.description: Develop the page component within the portal that will display the content library. This component will use the fetched user role to call the Sanity data-fetching function and then render the list of role-specific guides.
subtasks.details: In a page component like `app/(portal)/guides/page.tsx`, get the user's role. Call the `getGuidesByRole` function with the role. Map over the returned array of guides to render them as cards or a list, displaying fields like `title`, `summary`, and a link to the full guide.
subtasks.testStrategy: Log in as a user with the 'coach' role. Navigate to the guides page in the portal. Verify that the UI correctly renders only the guides intended for coaches. Check that the layout is clean and all expected data points are visible.

subtasks.title: Pass Role via Props and Handle Empty States
subtasks.description: Finalize the data flow by ensuring the user's role, fetched in the portal's root layout, is correctly passed down to child page components. Implement UI feedback for cases where no content is available for a user's role.
subtasks.details: In the `app/(portal)/layout.tsx`, fetch the user's role and pass it to `{children}`. The page component will receive this role and use it for data fetching. If the Sanity query returns an empty array, the page should render a user-friendly message, such as 'No content is currently available for your role.'
subtasks.testStrategy: Log in as a user whose role has no corresponding guides in Sanity. Verify that the page displays the 'no content available' message instead of an error or a blank section. Confirm that users with content see the content as expected.

tasks.title: Admin Feature: Bulk User Import from CSV
tasks.description: Develop a secure admin interface for bulk-importing licensed coaches from a CSV file. This process will create user accounts in Clerk and corresponding user records in Supabase.
tasks.details: Create an admin-only page, protected by checking for the 'admin' role. Add a file upload component for the CSV. On the backend, parse the CSV file. For each row, use the Clerk Backend API to create a user with an invitation. Then, use the Supabase client to create a corresponding record in the `users` table, storing the new Clerk ID, setting the role to 'coach', and linking the `club_id` if applicable.
tasks.testStrategy: Log in as an admin. Upload a sample CSV with coach data. Verify that invitation emails are sent via Clerk. Check the Clerk and Supabase dashboards to confirm that the new user records have been created correctly with the 'coach' role.

subtasks.title: Create Secure Admin Page for Bulk Import
subtasks.description: Develop a new page accessible only to users with the 'admin' role. This page will serve as the interface for the bulk user import functionality.
subtasks.details: Create a new route, for example `/admin/import-users`. Implement server-side logic to check the current user's session and metadata from Clerk. If the user's public metadata does not contain `role: 'admin'`, redirect them or show an 'Access Denied' message.
subtasks.testStrategy: Log in as a non-admin user and attempt to access the `/admin/import-users` URL; verify redirection or access denial. Log in as an admin user and verify the page loads successfully.

subtasks.title: Implement CSV File Upload Component
subtasks.description: Add a user-friendly file upload component to the admin import page that accepts only CSV files and posts the data to a backend endpoint.
subtasks.details: On the admin page, add a form with an `<input type="file" accept=".csv">`. Use client-side JavaScript to provide immediate feedback and enable a submit button only when a valid CSV file is selected. tasks.title: Build BTPP Website Home Page
tasks.description: Design and implement the main public-facing home page for the BTPP website, styled as a modern, distraction-free landing page. This page will serve as the primary entry point to the talent pool program, featuring a hero section without the main site header, filterable news, a section for national team exhibition games, and a comprehensive footer.
tasks.details:
The home page will be built as the root page (`/`) in the Next.js application, following a clean, modern, and conversion-focused style. It will be a statically generated public page that fetches dynamic content from Sanity. The page must be fully responsive and adhere to the established Defensie design system.

Key architectural requirement: The main site header navigation must be hidden on the home page to create a focused, immersive landing experience. The header will be visible on all other inner pages of the site.

The page structure will include:
1. **Hero Section:** A full-width section at the top with a compelling background, a primary headline about the talent pool program, a supportive sub-headline, a prominent Call-to-Action (CTA) button, and a secondary "Staff Login" button linking to `/staff-login`.
2. **Talent Pool Categories Section:** A prominent section featuring clickable category cards for U14, U16, National Teams U18, and U20. Each card will link to a dedicated category page (e.g., `/talentpools/[category]`)
3. **News Section:** A dynamic section that fetches recent `post` documents from all categories, with a filtering mechanism to sort by category.
4. **Exhibition Games Section:** A new section dedicated to showcasing upcoming and past exhibition games for the National Teams (U18, U20). It will display match details, results, and links to ticketing or registration information where available.
5. **Footer:** A comprehensive, multi-column footer containing quick links (Home, News, Categories), contact information, social media links, legal links (Privacy Policy, Terms of Service), and club/organization details.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Load the home page (`/`) and verify that the main site header/navigation is NOT visible.
2. Navigate to an inner page (e.g., a news article) and verify the main site header IS visible.
3. On the home page, verify that all sections (Hero, Talent Pool Categories, News, Exhibition Games, Footer) render correctly without layout issues on desktop, tablet, and mobile viewports.
4. Confirm the main CTA button and the "Staff Login" button in the hero section link to their correct destinations (e.g., sign-up page and `/staff-login` respectively).
5. Verify that the links in the 'Talent Pool Categories' section navigate to the correct dynamic category pages.
6. Test the 'News' section functionality, including the category filters and 'Read More' links.
7. Test the 'Exhibition Games' section: confirm it correctly fetches and displays upcoming and past games from Sanity, and that any ticket links are functional.
8. Check all links in the footer to ensure they point to the correct destinations.
9. Run a Lighthouse audit to check for performance, accessibility, and SEO best practices.
