tasks.title: Define and Implement Sanity Content Schemas for Talent Pool System
tasks.description: Create and update Sanity schemas for the talent pool system, including new types for categories and events, and linking them to existing posts. This task also involves creating the initial category documents and deploying the updated Sanity studio.
tasks.details:
1. **Create `talentPoolCategory` Schema:** In the Sanity schema directory, create a new file for `talentPoolCategory`. Define the schema with the following fields: `title` (string), `slug` (slug, with source set to 'title'), `description` (text), and `heroImage` (image with hotspot enabled).
2. **Update `post` Schema:** Modify the existing `post` schema file. Add a new reference field named `talentPoolCategory` that points to the `talentPoolCategory` type. This will allow news articles to be associated with a specific talent pool.
3. **Create `event` Schema:** Create a new schema file for `event`. Define it with fields for `title` (string), `description` (text), `date` (datetime), `location` (string), `imageUrl` (image), and a `category` field (reference to `talentPoolCategory`).
4. **Register New Schemas:** Add the new `talentPoolCategory` and `event` schemas to the `schema.types` array in your `sanity.config.ts` or `deskStructure.ts` file to make them active in the studio.
5. **Deploy Studio:** Deploy the updated Sanity Studio using the `sanity deploy` command to make the new schemas and fields live.
6. **Create Initial Documents:** After deployment, log into the Sanity Studio and manually create four `talentPoolCategory` documents: 'U14', 'U16', 'U18', and 'U20', ensuring their slugs are generated correctly.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. After deploying the studio, log in and verify that 'Talent Pool Category' and 'Event' appear as new content types in the studio's navigation pane.
2. Attempt to create a new 'Talent Pool Category' document. Confirm that all fields (title, slug, description, heroImage) are present and function as expected.
3. Edit an existing 'Post' document. Verify that a new 'Talent Pool Category' reference field is visible and allows you to select one of the categories created in the previous step.
4. Create a new 'Event' document. Confirm all fields are present and that you can successfully link it to a 'Talent Pool Category'.
5. Verify that the four initial category documents (U14, U16, U18, U20) exist in the studio and can be queried via the API.

subtasks.title: Create `talentPoolCategory` Schema Definition
subtasks.description: Define and implement the schema for 'Talent Pool Categories'. This schema will be used to categorize talent pool related content like posts and events.
subtasks.details: In the Sanity project's schema directory, create a new file named `talentPoolCategory.ts`. Define a document schema with `name: 'talentPoolCategory'` and `title: 'Talent Pool Category'`. Add the following fields: `title` (type: 'string'), `slug` (type: 'slug', with options: `{ source: 'title' }`), `description` (type: 'text'), and `heroImage` (type: 'image', with options: `{ hotspot: true }`).
subtasks.testStrategy: Run the local Sanity Studio using `sanity dev`. Check the terminal for any schema validation errors. The new type will not be visible in the studio UI until it's registered in a later step.

subtasks.title: Create `event` Schema Definition
subtasks.description: Define and implement the schema for 'Events'. Events will be associated with a specific talent pool category.
subtasks.details: In the schema directory, create a new file named `event.ts`. Define a document schema with `name: 'event'` and `title: 'Event'`. Add fields for `title` (string), `description` (text), `date` (datetime), `location` (string), `imageUrl` (image), and a `category` field which is a reference to the `talentPoolCategory` type (`type: 'reference', to: [{ type: 'talentPoolCategory' }]`).
subtasks.testStrategy: Run the local Sanity Studio using `sanity dev`. Check the terminal for any schema validation errors related to the new event schema or its reference to `talentPoolCategory`.

subtasks.title: Update `post` Schema and Register New Schema Types
subtasks.description: Modify the existing `post` schema to allow linking to a `talentPoolCategory` and register both the new `talentPoolCategory` and `event` schemas to make them active in the studio.
subtasks.details: 1. Open the existing `post` schema file. Add a new field named `talentPoolCategory` defined as a reference to the `talentPoolCategory` type (`type: 'reference', to: [{ type: 'talentPoolCategory' }]`). Make this field optional. 2. Open your main schema configuration file (e.g., `sanity.config.ts` or `schema.ts`). Import the `talentPoolCategory` and `event` schemas and add them to the `schema.types` array.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run the local Sanity Studio. Verify that 'Talent Pool Category' and 'Event' now appear as new, creatable content types in the studio's navigation pane. Open an existing 'Post' document and confirm the new 'Talent Pool Category' reference field is present and functional.

subtasks.title: Deploy Updated Sanity Studio
subtasks.description: Deploy the schema changes to the hosted Sanity Studio environment, making the new content types and fields available for content editors.
subtasks.details: After committing all local schema changes to your version control system, run the `sanity deploy` command in your terminal from the Sanity project directory. Follow the CLI prompts to build and deploy the studio.
subtasks.testStrategy: Once the deployment is complete, navigate to the production URL of your Sanity Studio. Log in and verify that the 'Talent Pool Category' and 'Event' content types are present. Confirm that the `talentPoolCategory` reference field is visible and usable within the 'Post' content type.

subtasks.title: Create Initial Talent Pool Category Documents
subtasks.description: Log into the deployed Sanity Studio and create the initial set of `talentPoolCategory` documents required for the application.
subtasks.details: Access the live Sanity Studio. Navigate to the 'Talent Pool Category' section. Create and publish four new documents with the following titles: 'U14', 'U16', 'U18', and 'U20'. Ensure the slug for each is generated correctly from the title before publishing.
subtasks.testStrategy: After creating the documents, use the Vision plugin in the Sanity Studio or a frontend query to fetch all documents of type `talentPoolCategory`. Verify that the four new categories exist and their data (title, slug) is correct.

tasks.title: Implement Staff Authentication and Role-Based Redirection
tasks.description: Create a dedicated, professional sign-in system for staff (admins, coaches, club owners). This includes a new login page, role-based redirection to appropriate dashboards upon successful authentication, and password reset functionality. Access will be provided via a button on the home page's hero section and a link in the header on all other pages.
tasks.details: Implement a new authentication flow specifically for non-player users. Create a new page at `/staff-login` using Clerk's `<SignIn>` component, styled with a professional and clean design to distinguish it from the main user sign-up. Configure the component's `afterSignInUrl` to point to a new page or API route (e.g., `/auth/redirect`) that will handle role-based redirection. This redirection logic will fetch the authenticated user's role from the Supabase `users` table (using the pattern established in Task #7) and redirect them accordingly: 'admin' to `/admin/dashboard`, 'coach' to `/portal/curriculum`, and 'club-owner' to a future `/portal/club-management` page. A simple placeholder page should be created at `/admin/dashboard` to serve as the admin landing page. Update the global header component to conditionally display a 'Staff Login' link; this link should be visible on all inner pages but hidden on the home page. Ensure the password reset functionality within the Clerk component is correctly configured and styled for this page.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. Verify the 'Staff Login' button on the home page (implemented in another task) navigates to `/staff-login`. Verify the 'Staff Login' link is visible in the header on an inner page (e.g., `/portal/curriculum`) and correctly navigates to `/staff-login`. Confirm the link is NOT present in the header on the home page. 2. Confirm the login page has a unique, professional design. 3. Log in as a user with the 'admin' role (created via Task #8) and verify you are redirected to `/admin/dashboard`. 4. Log out and log back in as a user with the 'coach' role. Verify you are redirected to the curriculum portal (e.g., `/portal/curriculum`). 5. Test the password reset flow from the `/staff-login` page to ensure it functions correctly for staff accounts. 6. While logged in as a coach, attempt to manually navigate to `/admin/dashboard` and confirm access is denied.

subtasks.title: Create the `/staff-login` Page with the Clerk `<SignIn>` Component
subtasks.description: Create a new page route at `/staff-login` in the Next.js application. Add the standard Clerk `<SignIn>` component to this page to provide the basic login functionality. This will serve as the foundation for the staff-specific authentication flow.
subtasks.details: Using the Next.js App Router, create a `page.tsx` file within a new `app/staff-login/` directory. Import and render the `<SignIn>` component from `@clerk/nextjs`. At this initial stage, do not configure custom redirection or styling.
subtasks.testStrategy: Navigate to the `/staff-login` URL in your browser. Verify that the default, unstyled Clerk sign-in form is rendered and functional.

subtasks.title: Implement Conditional 'Staff Login' Link in Header
subtasks.description: Update the global header component to conditionally display a 'Staff Login' link. This link should appear on all internal pages but be hidden on the main home page, where a dedicated button will be used instead.
subtasks.details: Modify the global header component. Use the `usePathname` hook from `next/navigation` to determine the current route. Conditionally render a Next.js `<Link>` component with the text 'Staff Login' and an `href` of `/staff-login` only when the pathname is not the root ('/'). Ensure its styling is consistent with other navigation elements.
subtasks.testStrategy: Navigate to an inner page (e.g., a news article or curriculum page) and verify the 'Staff Login' link is visible and functional in the header. Navigate to the home page and verify the link is not present in the header.

subtasks.title: Apply Custom Professional Styling to the Staff Login Page
subtasks.description: Apply custom CSS using Tailwind CSS to style the `/staff-login` page and the embedded Clerk `<SignIn>` component. The design must be professional, clean, and visually distinct from the main user sign-in page to clearly separate the user and staff authentication experiences.
subtasks.details: Use the `appearance` prop on the `<SignIn>` component to customize its elements (card, buttons, inputs) via Clerk's variable system or custom classes. Style the surrounding page layout (e.g., centering the form, adding a background, or a company logo). Ensure the integrated password reset flow inherits the same professional styling.
subtasks.testStrategy: Visually inspect the `/staff-login` page to confirm the custom styling is applied and looks distinct from `/sign-in`. Trigger the 'Forgot Password?' flow to ensure its modals/pages are also styled correctly.

subtasks.title: Create the Placeholder Admin Dashboard Page
subtasks.description: Create a new, simple page at `/admin/dashboard`. This page will serve as the landing destination for users with the 'admin' role after they successfully authenticate through the staff login.
subtasks.details:
Create a new route by adding a `page.tsx` file inside an `app/admin/dashboard/` directory. The page should be a server component that contains a simple heading like `

Admin Dashboard

` and some placeholder text. This route will be protected in a later step.
subtasks.testStrategy: Manually navigate to `/admin/dashboard` (while logged in with any account for now) and verify that the placeholder page with the title 'Admin Dashboard' is rendered.

subtasks.title: Implement the `/auth/redirect` Handler for Role-Based Logic
subtasks.description: Create a new server-side page or API route at `/auth/redirect` that will handle the post-login redirection. This handler will fetch the authenticated user's role from the Supabase `users` table and redirect them to the appropriate dashboard.
subtasks.details: Create a new route handler (e.g., `app/auth/redirect/page.tsx` as a server component). Use Clerk's `auth()` helper to get the `userId`. Use the Supabase client to query the `users` table for the record matching the Clerk `userId`. Based on the retrieved `role` ('admin', 'coach', 'club-owner'), use the `redirect` function from `next/navigation` to send the user to the correct path (`/admin/dashboard`, `/portal/curriculum`, `/portal/club-management`). Include a fallback redirect to a default page.
subtasks.testStrategy: This route is hard to test in isolation. Its functionality will be verified in the next step. You can add `console.log` statements to verify role fetching during development.

subtasks.title: Configure Clerk `<SignIn>` to Use the Redirection Handler
subtasks.description: Update the `<SignIn>` component on the `/staff-login` page to use the new `/auth/redirect` handler after a successful sign-in, connecting the login UI to the role-based redirection logic.
subtasks.details: In the `app/staff-login/page.tsx` file, modify the `<SignIn>` component. Add the `afterSignInUrl` prop and set its value to `/auth/redirect`. This tells Clerk to send the user to your custom handler instead of its default destination.
subtasks.testStrategy: Using a test account with the 'admin' role, log in via the `/staff-login` page. Verify you are successfully redirected to `/admin/dashboard`. Repeat for a 'coach' role, verifying redirection to `/portal/curriculum`.

subtasks.title: Secure Staff Routes via Middleware and Finalize Testing
subtasks.description: Update the application's middleware to protect the new staff-specific routes. Perform a full end-to-end test of the entire staff authentication and redirection flow for all defined roles to ensure security and correctness.
subtasks.details: In `middleware.ts`, add `/admin/dashboard` and the future `/portal/club-management` to the list of protected routes. Ensure the middleware correctly redirects any unauthenticated users attempting to access these routes to the appropriate login page (e.g., `/staff-login`).
subtasks.testStrategy: 1. Log out and attempt to navigate directly to `/admin/dashboard`; verify you are redirected to a login page. 2. Log in as an 'admin' and confirm successful redirection and access. 3. Log in as a 'coach' and confirm redirection. 4. Confirm a regular player user cannot access `/admin/dashboard` (they should be redirected away).

tasks.title: Enhance Talent Pool Category Cards with Images and Hover Effects
tasks.description: Update the talent pool category cards on the home page to be more visually engaging. This involves incorporating background images for each category, increasing card size, and adding interactive hover effects for animation and information display.
tasks.details: Locate the component responsible for rendering the list of talent pool categories on the home page (created in Task 11). Modify this component to fetch and display the `heroImage` for each category from Sanity (schema defined in Task 13). The image should be used as the card's background. Increase the vertical height of the cards to better accommodate the images. Implement the following CSS/Tailwind styles: 1. **Default State:** Apply a grayscale filter (`filter: grayscale(100%)`) and a slight dimming effect to the card. The category title should be visible. 2. **Hover State:** On hover, smoothly transition the card to full color (remove grayscale) and apply a 'pop out' effect using `transform: scale(1.05)`. The category description, which is hidden by default, should fade in and become visible on hover. Ensure all transitions are smooth.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. Navigate to the website's home page and locate the talent pool categories section. 2. Verify that each category card is vertically larger than before and displays a background image. 3. Confirm that in their default, non-hovered state, the cards appear grayed out and only the category title is visible. 4. Hover your mouse over a card. 5. Verify the card smoothly transitions to full color. 6. Verify the card animates outwards slightly (the 'pop out' effect). 7. Verify the category description text fades in and becomes readable. 8. Move the mouse off the card and confirm it smoothly returns to its grayed-out default state and the description disappears. 9. Check this functionality across multiple cards to ensure it's dynamic. 10. Use browser developer tools to confirm the new layout is responsive on mobile and tablet screen sizes.

subtasks.title: Update GROQ Query to Fetch Category `heroImage`
subtasks.description: Modify the Sanity GROQ query used on the home page to include the `heroImage` field for each talent pool category. This will make the image URL available to the React component for rendering.
subtasks.details: Locate the data fetching function responsible for retrieving the list of `talentPoolCategory` documents for the home page. In the GROQ query's projection, add the `heroImage` field with a reference to its asset URL. The updated projection should look something like: `*[_type == "talentPoolCategory"]{ title, slug, description, 'heroImage': heroImage.asset->url }`.
subtasks.testStrategy: After implementing the change, log the fetched data to the browser console. Verify that each category object in the resulting array now contains a `heroImage` key with a valid Sanity CDN URL.

subtasks.title: Update Card Component for Background Image and Increased Size
subtasks.description: Modify the talent pool category card component to use the fetched `heroImage` URL as a background image. Concurrently, increase the card's vertical height to better accommodate the image and the content that will be displayed on hover.
subtasks.details: In the React component that renders each category card, apply the fetched `heroImage` URL as an inline style for `backgroundImage`. Use Tailwind CSS classes to set a new, larger height (e.g., `h-80`). Also apply `bg-cover` and `bg-center` to ensure the image fills the card area appropriately. The card container should have `position: relative` to prepare for overlay content.
subtasks.testStrategy: Navigate to the home page. Confirm that each category card is now taller and displays its unique background image. The images should cover the entire card area without distortion.

subtasks.title: Implement Default Card State Styles (Grayscale & Dimmed)
subtasks.description: Apply the default, non-hovered CSS styles to the category cards. This includes applying a grayscale filter, adding a semi-transparent overlay to dim the image, and ensuring the category description is hidden by default.
subtasks.details: On the main card container, add the `grayscale` and `transition-all duration-300 ease-in-out` Tailwind classes. Create a child `div` inside the card to act as an overlay, styling it with `absolute inset-0 bg-black/30`. Ensure the category description element is rendered but hidden using the `opacity-0` class. The category title should remain visible on top of the overlay.
subtasks.testStrategy: Load the home page and observe the category cards. They should all appear in grayscale with a noticeable dimming effect. Only the category title should be visible; the description should be hidden.

subtasks.title: Implement Card Hover Effects (Color, Scale, Fade-in)
subtasks.description: Implement the interactive hover effects using CSS. On hover, the card should smoothly transition to full color, scale up slightly, and the hidden description should fade into view.
subtasks.details: Use Tailwind's `group` and `group-hover:` utilities. Add the `group` class to the main card container. Then, apply the following hover variants: `hover:grayscale-0` and `hover:scale-105` to the card container. For the description element, use `group-hover:opacity-100` to make it fade in. The `transition` classes added in the previous subtask will animate these property changes.
subtasks.testStrategy: Hover your mouse over a category card. Verify that it smoothly animates to full color and grows slightly larger. The category description should fade in clearly. When the mouse is moved off the card, it should smoothly return to its default grayscale state.

subtasks.title: Responsive Testing and Refinement
subtasks.description: Thoroughly test the newly designed category cards across different screen sizes, including mobile, tablet, and desktop, to ensure the layout, image display, and hover effects are fully responsive and user-friendly.
subtasks.details: Use browser developer tools to test the home page at various common breakpoints (e.g., 375px, 768px, 1024px, 1440px). Check for any layout breaks, text overflow, or undesirable image cropping. Adjust styles using responsive prefixes (e.g., `md:h-96`) if necessary. On touch devices, the hover effect will trigger on tap; ensure this behavior does not impede navigation to the category page.
subtasks.testStrategy: 1. Verify the card grid layout adapts correctly on mobile (single column) and tablet (two columns). 2. Confirm that text remains readable and does not overflow the card boundaries on any screen size. 3. On a touch device or simulator, tap a card to confirm it navigates to the correct URL and the hover state doesn't create a poor user experience.

tasks.title: Finalize Sanity Schemas and Populate Initial Content
tasks.description: Conduct a comprehensive review and correction of all Sanity schemas (post, event, guide, etc.) to align with frontend requirements, and populate the CMS with initial data for all content types to establish a complete content foundation.
tasks.details: 1. **Schema Review and Validation**: Review existing schemas: `talentPoolCategory`, `event`, `post`, `exhibitionGame`, `guide`, and `page`. Validate all fields against frontend data requirements and GROQ queries. Add missing fields, such as `slug` where needed for routing. 2. **Schema Corrections**: Update the `event` schema by renaming `date` to `eventDate`, `location` to `venue`, and `imageUrl` to `mainImage`. Update the `post` schema by renaming `featuredImage` to `mainImage` and ensuring the `categories` reference field is correctly configured. 3. **Initial Data Population**: Create initial documents in Sanity. For `talentPoolCategory`, create U14, U16, U18, and U20. Create sample `event`, `post`, `exhibitionGame`, and `guide` documents, ensuring they are populated with realistic data. 4. **Data Relationships**: Link the newly created documents. Ensure sample events and posts are correctly referenced to their respective talent pool categories. Verify all reference fields function as expected in the studio.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Query Validation**: Execute all existing GROQ queries against the populated Sanity dataset and verify they return the expected data structure with the corrected field names. 2. **Frontend Verification**: Navigate to all pages that consume Sanity data (News, Talent Pool pages, Curriculum, etc.). Confirm that all content displays correctly without errors. Pay special attention to images, dates, and venues on event and post listings. 3. **Relationship Testing**: Verify that navigating from a category page to a linked post or event works correctly. Ensure that filtering content by category on the frontend functions as expected with the populated data.

subtasks.title: Review Schemas and Add Missing `slug` Fields
subtasks.description: Systematically review all existing Sanity schemas (`talentPoolCategory`, `event`, `post`, `exhibitionGame`, `guide`, `page`) to ensure they meet frontend data requirements. Add a `slug` field to any schema that will be used for generating unique page routes and doesn't already have one.
subtasks.details: For each relevant schema file (e.g., `schemas/post.js`), add a field definition like: `{ name: 'slug', title: 'Slug', type: 'slug', options: { source: 'title', maxLength: 96 }, validation: Rule => Rule.required() }`. Ensure this is implemented for `event`, `post`, `guide`, and `exhibitionGame` schemas to support unique page URLs.
subtasks.testStrategy: After implementation, run the Sanity Studio locally (`sanity start`) and check each specified content type to confirm the 'Slug' field appears and auto-generates from the 'Title' field.

subtasks.title: Rename Fields in `event` and `post` Schemas for Consistency
subtasks.description: Update the `event` and `post` schemas to rename fields as specified in the requirements. In the `event` schema, rename `date` to `eventDate`, `location` to `venue`, and `imageUrl` to `mainImage`. In the `post` schema, rename `featuredImage` to `mainImage`.
subtasks.details: Open the `schemas/event.js` file and change the `name` property of the relevant fields (e.g., change `{ name: 'date', ... }` to `{ name: 'eventDate', ... }`). Repeat for `location` -> `venue` and `imageUrl` -> `mainImage`. Then, in `schemas/post.js`, update the `featuredImage` field definition to `{ name: 'mainImage', ... }`. This will require a data migration script if production data exists, but for initial setup, this change is sufficient.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run the Sanity Studio locally and open the 'Event' and 'Post' forms. Verify that the field labels have been updated to 'Event Date', 'Venue', and 'Main Image' respectively.

subtasks.title: Validate and Correct Schema Reference Fields
subtasks.description: Review and correct all reference fields across the schemas, with a specific focus on the `categories` field in the `post` schema. Ensure it correctly references the `talentPoolCategory` type and is configured to allow multiple selections.
subtasks.details: In `schemas/post.js`, locate the `categories` field (or `talentPoolCategory` field as per Task 13). Ensure its definition allows for multiple references: `{ name: 'categories', title: 'Categories', type: 'array', of: [{ type: 'reference', to: {type: 'talentPoolCategory'} }] }`. Review `event` and `exhibitionGame` schemas and add a similar reference field to `talentPoolCategory` if it's missing.
subtasks.testStrategy: In the local Sanity Studio, open a 'Post' document. Confirm that the 'Categories' field allows you to search for and select multiple 'Talent Pool Category' items.

subtasks.title: Deploy Updated Schemas and Populate `talentPoolCategory` Documents
subtasks.description: Deploy all the modified Sanity schemas to the hosted Studio environment. After deployment is successful, log into the Studio and create the initial set of `talentPoolCategory` documents: U14, U16, U18, and U20.
subtasks.details: Run the `sanity deploy` command from the Sanity project directory to push all local schema changes to the hosted Sanity Studio. Once the deployment is complete, navigate to the Studio URL. Go to the 'Talent Pool Category' content type and create four new documents, setting their titles to 'U14', 'U16', 'U18', and 'U20'.
subtasks.testStrategy: Access the live Sanity Studio URL. Verify that all schema changes (new slugs, renamed fields, corrected references) are reflected. Confirm that the four new 'Talent Pool Category' documents are created and visible.

subtasks.title: Create Sample Documents for Core Content Types
subtasks.description: Populate the Sanity CMS with at least two sample documents for each of the following types: `post`, `event`, `guide`, and `exhibitionGame`. Use realistic placeholder text and images, ensuring all new and renamed fields are filled out.
subtasks.details: In the live Sanity Studio, create new documents for each content type. For posts, write a short article with a title and body. For events, specify a date using `eventDate`, a location in `venue`, and upload an image to `mainImage`. For guides, create content targeted at different roles ('coach', 'staff') to support Task 7. Ensure all documents have a `slug`.
subtasks.testStrategy: Browse the content lists for 'Post', 'Event', 'Guide', and 'Exhibition Game' in the Studio to ensure at least two documents exist for each and appear to be populated correctly.

subtasks.title: Link Sample Content and Verify Data Relationships
subtasks.description: Edit the sample `post`, `event`, and `exhibitionGame` documents to link them to the appropriate `talentPoolCategory` documents (U14, U16, etc.). Verify that the reference pickers in the Sanity Studio work as expected and that the relationships are saved correctly.
subtasks.details: Open a sample `post` document in the Studio. In the `categories` reference field, select one or more of the `talentPoolCategory` documents (e.g., U16, U18). Save the post. Repeat this process for other posts, events, and exhibition games, distributing them across the different categories to ensure varied test data for the frontend.
subtasks.testStrategy: Execute GROQ queries from the Sanity Vision plugin or the frontend application. For example, a query like `*[_type == 'post' && references(*[_type=='talentPoolCategory' && title == 'U16']._id)]` should return only the posts linked to the 'U16' category.

tasks.title: Redesign Talent Pool Category Cards with 3D Stacked Layout
tasks.description: Overhaul the TalentPoolCategories component with a modern 3D stacked card layout, enhanced hover interactions, and improved visual aesthetics to create a more engaging user experience.
tasks.details:
This task involves a complete redesign of the category cards in `/apps/web/components/sections/TalentPoolCategories.tsx`. 
1. **Layout & Sizing**: Modify the card container to remove the existing `gap-6`. Implement a stacked layout where cards overlap. This can be achieved by using absolute positioning for card elements within a relative container or by applying negative margins. Each subsequent card should be visually tucked behind the previous one using a combination of `z-index` and a `top` offset (e.g., `top: index * 30px`). The height of the cards should be significantly increased, approximately doubling the current `h-64 md:h-80`.
2. **3D Stacked Effect & Hover Interaction**: On hover, the target card should smoothly transition to a higher `z-index`, scale up slightly (e.g., `transform: scale(1.05)`), and move forward to appear above all other cards. Apply a more pronounced `box-shadow` during the hover state to enhance the 'pop-up' effect. All transformations should use CSS transitions for smoothness.
3. **Content & Styling**: Remove the 'View Details' button and its overlay entirely. The entire card is already wrapped in a Next.js `Link` component, so ensure this core functionality is preserved. The existing behavior where the description text fades in on hover should also be maintained. Enhance the gradient overlay on the images to ensure text remains readable. The grayscale-to-color filter transition on hover should be kept and refined for a better visual effect.
4. **Responsiveness**: Ensure the stacked layout is responsive. On smaller screens, the overlap effect may need to be reduced or adjusted to maintain usability and prevent content from becoming obscured.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Navigate to the home page and locate the Talent Pool Categories section. 
2. **Visual Verification**: Confirm that the cards are vertically stacked, overlapping each other, and have no vertical gap between them. Verify the cards are significantly taller than before. Check for a subtle shadow creating a depth effect.
3. **Hover Interaction**: Hover the mouse over each card in the stack, starting from the one on top. Verify that the hovered card smoothly animates, scales up, and moves to the foreground with a more prominent shadow. Confirm that the description text fades in as expected.
4. **Functionality**: Confirm the 'View Details' button has been completely removed. Click on each of the category cards and verify that it correctly navigates to the corresponding talent pool page (e.g., clicking 'U14' goes to '/talentpools/u14').
5. **Responsiveness**: Open browser developer tools and switch to mobile and tablet views (e.g., 375px, 768px). Verify that the stacked card layout adapts gracefully, remains visually appealing, and all cards are still interactive and clickable without UI bugs.

subtasks.title: Refactor Card Container for Stacking and Increase Card Height
subtasks.description: Prepare the foundational structure for the stacked layout. This involves modifying the parent container in `TalentPoolCategories.tsx` to support absolute positioning for its children and increasing the height of the individual cards to accommodate the new design.
subtasks.details: Navigate to `/apps/web/components/sections/TalentPoolCategories.tsx`. Locate the `div` that wraps the mapped category cards. Remove the `gap-6` class and add `relative` to establish a new positioning context. Then, for each card component being rendered, increase its height significantly using Tailwind classes, for example, from `h-64 md:h-80` to `h-[30rem] md:h-[36rem]`. This initial step ensures the container is ready and the cards have the necessary vertical space for the stacking effect.
subtasks.testStrategy: After implementation, view the component on the home page. The cards should now be much taller and will likely overlap at the top-left corner of the container since the gap is removed and no positioning has been applied yet. This confirms the basic structural changes are in place.

subtasks.title: Implement Static Vertical Stack Layout with Positional Offsets
subtasks.description: Create the static, non-interactive version of the 3D stacked layout. This involves applying absolute positioning to each card and dynamically calculating its `top` offset and `z-index` based on its position in the array.
subtasks.details: Inside the `.map()` function that renders the cards, apply `absolute w-full` to each card element. Calculate the vertical offset and stacking order based on the element's `index`. Set the `top` style property dynamically: `style={{ top: `${index * 40}px` }}`. Set the `z-index` in reverse order to ensure the first card appears on top initially: `style={{ zIndex: categories.length - index }}`. Combine these into a single style object.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify that the cards are rendered in a vertical stack, with each card partially obscured by the one before it. The first card in the list should be fully visible at the top of the stack. The layout should be static and not yet interactive.

subtasks.title: Develop Hover-Triggered Transformations and Z-Index Shift
subtasks.description: Implement the core hover interaction where a card 'pops out' from the stack. This involves using CSS transforms to scale the card and adjusting its z-index to bring it to the forefront, with smooth transitions.
subtasks.details: On the main wrapper of each card, add CSS transition properties: `transition-all duration-300 ease-in-out`. Use Tailwind's `hover:` variants to apply transformations. On hover, the card should scale up (`hover:scale-105`) and its z-index must be increased to appear above all other cards (`hover:z-50`). You may also add a slight vertical translation (`hover:-translate-y-4`) to enhance the effect.
subtasks.testStrategy: Hover over various cards in the stack. The hovered card should smoothly animate, growing in size and moving to the top of the stack, appearing above all other cards. When the mouse leaves, it should smoothly return to its original position.

subtasks.title: Refine Hover State Visuals: Shadow, Grayscale, and Text
subtasks.description: Enhance the visual feedback of the hover state. This includes adding a more pronounced box shadow, ensuring the existing grayscale-to-color filter transition is preserved, and confirming the description text fades in as intended.
subtasks.details: Add a default box-shadow to the card (e.g., `shadow-lg`) and a more pronounced one on hover (`hover:shadow-2xl`). Ensure the card's parent element has the `group` class. Verify that the existing `group-hover:grayscale-0` on the background image and `group-hover:opacity-100` on the description text container still function correctly with the new hover transformations.
subtasks.testStrategy: When hovering over a card, confirm that its shadow becomes deeper, the background image transitions from grayscale to full color, and the description text fades into view. All these effects should occur simultaneously with the transformations from the previous subtask.

subtasks.title: Remove 'View Details' Button and Finalize Link Behavior
subtasks.description: Streamline the card's content by removing the 'View Details' button and its overlay. Ensure the entire card area remains a single, clickable link that navigates to the correct category page.
subtasks.details: In the card's JSX, locate and delete the elements that render the 'View Details' button and the semi-transparent overlay it sits on. Confirm that the Next.js `<Link>` component wraps the entire card structure. Enhance the permanent gradient overlay on the image (e.g., `bg-gradient-to-t from-black/80 to-transparent`) to ensure the title and description text remain readable without the button's overlay.
subtasks.testStrategy: Visually confirm the 'View Details' button and its overlay are gone. Click on different parts of any card (image, title area, etc.). Each click should successfully navigate to the corresponding talent pool category page (e.g., `/talentpools/u16`).

subtasks.title: Implement Responsive Design for Smaller Screens
subtasks.description: Adapt the 3D stacked layout for mobile and tablet devices to ensure usability. The vertical overlap will be reduced or removed on smaller screens to prevent content from being inaccessible.
subtasks.details: Use Tailwind's responsive prefixes to adjust the layout. For mobile (default), revert to a simpler layout. Remove the `absolute` positioning and `top`/`z-index` styles. Instead, use `position: relative` and add a margin (e.g., `mb-4`) to create a simple vertical list. Re-introduce the stacked layout for larger screens using `md:absolute`. The `top` offset should also be responsive: `style={{ top: 0 }}` for mobile and `style={{ top: `${index * 40}px` }}` for `md` and up. This requires conditional styling logic.
subtasks.testStrategy: Load the page on a desktop and resize the browser window to be narrower than the `md` breakpoint (768px). The layout should transition from the 3D stack to a standard, non-overlapping vertical list of cards. Verify that hover effects and clickability are maintained in both layouts.

tasks.title: Implement Home Page Recruitment Banner
tasks.description: Create a new banner section on the public home page designed to attract new players. The banner will use a tertiary color scheme and feature a prominent call-to-action (CTA) to guide prospective members to registration or information pages.
tasks.details: 1. **Sanity Schema Update:** Modify the 'homePage' document schema in Sanity. Add a new field group for the recruitment banner with fields for a headline (string), body text (text), CTA button text (string), and a CTA link (URL or internal reference). 2. **Content Entry:** Populate the new banner fields in the Sanity Studio for the home page document. 3. **Component Development:** Create a new React component, `RecruitmentBanner.js`, in the Next.js application. This component will accept the banner content as props. 4. **Home Page Integration:** Update the main home page component (`/app/page.js`) to fetch the new banner data from Sanity. Pass the fetched data to the `RecruitmentBanner` component. 5. **Styling:** Style the banner using Tailwind CSS, ensuring it adheres to the specified tertiary color palette. The CTA button should have distinct hover and focus states to encourage interaction. The banner must be fully responsive.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. Navigate to the home page and verify the recruitment banner is displayed correctly. 2. Inspect the banner's styling to confirm it uses the project's tertiary color variables. 3. Test the banner's layout on various screen sizes (desktop, tablet, mobile) to ensure it remains visually appealing and functional. 4. Click the call-to-action button and confirm it navigates to the correct destination URL (e.g., the 'Tryout Info' page). 5. Log in to the Sanity Studio, modify the banner's headline and CTA text, publish the changes, and verify the live home page updates immediately or after a rebuild.

subtasks.title: Update Sanity Schema for Recruitment Banner
subtasks.description: Modify the 'homePage' document schema in the Sanity project to include fields for the new recruitment banner. This will enable content editors to manage the banner's content directly from the Sanity Studio.
subtasks.details: In the Sanity schema definition for the 'homePage' document type, add a new field of type 'object' named `recruitmentBanner`. Inside this object, define the following fields: `headline` (type: 'string'), `bodyText` (type: 'text'), `ctaButtonText` (type: 'string'), and `ctaLink` (type: 'url'). Group these fields logically within the schema for better organization in the Studio.
subtasks.testStrategy: After deploying the schema changes, open the Sanity Studio and navigate to the Home Page document. Verify that the new 'Recruitment Banner' fields (Headline, Body Text, CTA Button Text, CTA Link) are present and editable.

subtasks.title: Enter Recruitment Banner Content in Sanity
subtasks.description: Use the Sanity Studio to populate the newly created fields for the recruitment banner on the home page document. This provides the actual content that will be displayed on the website.
subtasks.details: Log in to the Sanity Studio. Navigate to the 'Home Page' document. Fill in the `recruitmentBanner` fields with appropriate content: a compelling headline, descriptive body text, a clear call-to-action for the button text (e.g., 'Join Now' or 'Learn More'), and the target URL for the CTA button (e.g., '/sign-up'). Publish the changes.
subtasks.testStrategy: Confirm that the content is saved and published correctly in Sanity. Re-opening the document should show the entered values. Check the API response to ensure the new data is available.

subtasks.title: Develop the RecruitmentBanner React Component
subtasks.description: Create a new, reusable React component that will render the structure of the recruitment banner. This component will be stateless and receive all its data via props.
subtasks.details: In the Next.js project's components directory, create a new file `RecruitmentBanner.js`. Define a functional component that accepts a `bannerData` prop. This prop will be an object containing `headline`, `bodyText`, `ctaButtonText`, and `ctaLink`. The component should render the basic HTML structure: a `section` containing an `h2` for the headline, a `p` for the body text, and a Next.js `<Link>` component for the CTA button. Focus on structure and props, not final styling.
subtasks.testStrategy: Use Storybook or a temporary test page to render the component with mock data. Verify that it correctly displays the headline, text, and that the button has the correct text and `href` attribute.

subtasks.title: Fetch Sanity Data and Integrate Banner on Home Page
subtasks.description: Update the main home page to fetch the recruitment banner content from Sanity and render the `RecruitmentBanner` component with the fetched data.
subtasks.details:
In the home page file (`/app/page.js`), modify the existing Sanity query to include the `recruitmentBanner` object and its fields. The GROQ query should look something like `*[_type == "homePage"][0]{..., recruitmentBanner{headline, bodyText, ctaButtonText, ctaLink}}`. In the page component, receive the data and pass the `recruitmentBanner` object as a prop to the `<RecruitmentBanner />` component. Conditionally render the banner only if the `recruitmentBanner` data exists.
<info added on 2025-08-01T12:03:32.815Z>
The data fetching from Sanity must be performed using server-side rendering (SSR) in Next.js. The GROQ query should be executed on the server, with the resulting data passed down to the component as props. This method ensures the banner content is available on the initial page load, which is crucial for SEO.
</info added on 2025-08-01T12:03:32.815Z>
subtasks.testStrategy: Load the home page in the development environment. Verify that the data is fetched correctly from Sanity by logging the data or using React DevTools. Confirm that the unstyled `RecruitmentBanner` component renders on the page with the content from Sanity.

subtasks.title: Apply Responsive Styling to the Recruitment Banner
subtasks.description: Apply Tailwind CSS classes to the `RecruitmentBanner` component to match the design specifications, including the tertiary color scheme, typography, and responsive layouts.
subtasks.details: Edit the `RecruitmentBanner.js` component. Use Tailwind CSS utility classes to style the banner section, headline, body text, and CTA button. Implement the project's tertiary color palette for the background and text. Style the CTA button to be prominent, with distinct `:hover` and `:focus` states (e.g., change in background color or a subtle transform). Use responsive prefixes (e.g., `md:`, `lg:`) to ensure the layout adapts correctly to desktop, tablet, and mobile screen sizes.
subtasks.testStrategy: View the home page on different screen sizes using browser developer tools. Verify the banner is fully responsive and the layout doesn't break. Check that the colors match the tertiary color scheme defined in `tailwind.config.js`. Test the CTA button's hover and focus states for visual feedback.

tasks.title: Create Coach Recruitment Banner and Content Page
tasks.description: Develop a new recruitment banner targeting coaches, to be placed on the public home page. This task also includes creating the internal, coach-specific landing page that the banner's call-to-action will link to.
tasks.details: 1. **Sanity Schema Update:** Modify the 'homePage' document schema in Sanity. Add a new field group for the coach recruitment banner with fields for a headline (string), body text (text), CTA button text (string), and a reference to the internal page. This should be modeled after the player recruitment banner schema from Task 18. 2. **Create Internal Page:** Implement a new page at a secure route like `/portal/coaching-resources`. This page must be protected and only accessible to users with the 'coach' role, using the role-based access control established in Task 7. 3. **Page Content:** The new page will display content relevant to coaches. It should fetch `guide` documents from Sanity where `targetRole` is 'coach'. Consider adding a new category field to guides to specifically pull content for 'coaching events', 'degree information', etc. 4. **Banner Component:** Create or adapt the existing `RecruitmentBanner` component to display the new coach-focused content. Ensure it is styled using the project's tertiary color variables. 5. **Integration:** Add the new banner component to the main public-facing layout (e.g., home page), fetching its content from Sanity. The CTA button must link to the new `/portal/coaching-resources` page.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Sanity Verification:** After schema deployment, log into the Sanity Studio. Verify the new fields for the coach banner exist on the home page document and populate them. Create at least two `guide` documents tagged for the 'coach' role and assign them to a 'coaching-resources' category. 2. **Public Banner Test:** As an unauthenticated user, view the home page and confirm the coach banner is displayed correctly with the specified text and tertiary colors. 3. **CTA and Auth Flow:** Click the banner's CTA. Verify it redirects to the login page. After logging in as a 'coach', verify you are correctly forwarded to the `/portal/coaching-resources` page. 4. **Authenticated Access:** Log in as a 'coach' and navigate to the `/portal/coaching-resources` page. Confirm the correct guides are displayed. 5. **Role Gating:** Log in as a user with a non-coach role (e.g., 'staff') and attempt to access `/portal/coaching-resources` directly. Verify access is denied or the user is redirected.

subtasks.title: Update Sanity Schemas for Coach Banner and Guide Categorization
subtasks.description: Modify the Sanity schemas to support the new coach recruitment banner content on the home page and to allow for better categorization of `guide` documents for the new landing page.
subtasks.details: In the Sanity project, locate the 'homePage' document schema. Add a new field group named `coachRecruitmentBanner`. Within this group, add the following fields: `headline` (string), `bodyText` (text), and `ctaText` (string). Next, modify the 'guide' document schema to include a new `category` field (string) with a predefined list of options, including 'coaching-resources', 'coaching-events', and 'degree-information'. Deploy these schema changes to the Sanity Studio.
subtasks.testStrategy: After deployment, log into the Sanity Studio. Open the 'homePage' document and verify the new 'Coach Recruitment Banner' field group and its sub-fields are present and editable. Then, create or edit a 'guide' document and confirm the new 'category' dropdown field is available and functional.

subtasks.title: Create Secure Route and Page for Coaching Resources
subtasks.description: Implement the new page at the `/portal/coaching-resources` route and secure it so that it is only accessible to authenticated users who have the 'coach' role.
subtasks.details: Create a new Next.js page file at `app/(portal)/coaching-resources/page.tsx`. This route falls under the `(portal)` group which should already be protected by Clerk middleware (from Task 4). Inside the page component, implement a server-side check to get the current user's role from Supabase (as established in Task 7). If the user's role is not 'coach', either redirect them to the portal dashboard or render an 'Access Denied' component. For now, the page can contain a simple placeholder message like 'Coaching Resources Page'.
subtasks.testStrategy: Log in as a user with the 'coach' role and navigate to `/portal/coaching-resources`; verify the page loads. Log in as a user with a different role (e.g., 'staff') and confirm you are redirected or see an access denied message. Attempt to access the URL while logged out and confirm you are redirected to the sign-in page.

subtasks.title: Fetch and Display Coach-Specific Guides on Resources Page
subtasks.description: Enhance the `/portal/coaching-resources` page to fetch and display all `guide` documents from Sanity that are specifically targeted at coaches.
subtasks.details: On the `coaching-resources` page component, implement a Sanity client query to fetch content. Use a GROQ query to select all documents of type `guide` where the `targetRole` field is equal to 'coach'. Map over the returned array of guides and render them as a list or a grid of cards. Each card should display at least the guide's `title` and `summary`, and link to the full guide page.
subtasks.testStrategy: In Sanity, create at least three `guide` documents. Set `targetRole` to 'coach' for two of them and 'staff' for the third. Log in as a coach, navigate to the resources page, and verify that only the two coach-specific guides are displayed. Check that the links on the cards are correct.

subtasks.title: Adapt RecruitmentBanner Component for Coach Content
subtasks.description: Adapt the existing `RecruitmentBanner` component (from Task 18) or create a new one to display the coach-focused content, ensuring it is styled with the project's tertiary color scheme.
subtasks.details: Locate the existing `RecruitmentBanner` component. Ensure it is flexible enough to accept `headline`, `bodyText`, `ctaText`, and `ctaLink` as props. Add a new style variant or prop (e.g., `variant='coach'`) that applies the project's defined tertiary color variables for the background or accent colors. This will visually distinguish it from the primary player recruitment banner.
subtasks.testStrategy: Use Storybook or a temporary test page to render the banner component. Pass it mock data for the coach variant and verify it displays all text content correctly. Confirm that the component's styling correctly applies the tertiary colors and is distinct from other banner variants.

subtasks.title: Integrate Coach Banner on Home Page with Sanity Data
subtasks.description: Add the newly styled coach recruitment banner to the public home page, fetching its content dynamically from Sanity and linking its CTA button to the new resources page.
subtasks.details: In the main public home page component (e.g., `app/page.tsx`), update the Sanity query to also fetch the `coachRecruitmentBanner` field group from the `homePage` document. Import the `RecruitmentBanner` component from subtask 19.4. Render the component on the page, passing the fetched Sanity data (`headline`, `bodyText`, `ctaText`) as props. Hardcode the `ctaLink` prop to `/portal/coaching-resources`.
subtasks.testStrategy: In the Sanity Studio, populate the coach banner fields on the `homePage` document and publish the changes. Load the application's public home page and verify the coach banner appears with the correct content. Click the CTA button and confirm it navigates to `/portal/coaching-resources` (which should then prompt for login if the user is not an authenticated coach).

tasks.title: Implement Role-Based Coach Dashboard
tasks.description: Create a centralized dashboard for authenticated users with the 'coach' role. This dashboard will serve as the main landing page within the secure portal for coaches, displaying private content such as guides, rule changes, and other non-public resources.
tasks.details: Implement a new page at the route `/portal/dashboard`. This page will become the primary landing area for users with the 'coach' role after they log in. The component should leverage the role-based access control established in Task 7, fetching the user's role from Supabase. The dashboard layout should use a grid system to display multiple content widgets, such as 'Latest Announcements', 'Featured Guides', and 'Upcoming Rule Changes'. To support this, a new schema for `announcement` should be created in Sanity with fields for a title, date, and body content, and it must include a `targetRole` field. The dashboard page will perform multiple GROQ queries to fetch `guide` and `announcement` documents from Sanity where `targetRole` is 'coach'.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. Log in as a user with the 'coach' role and verify you are automatically redirected to and can access `/portal/dashboard`. 2. Log in as a user with a different role (e.g., 'staff') and confirm you cannot access the coach dashboard. 3. As an unauthenticated user, attempt to navigate to `/portal/dashboard` and verify you are redirected to the login page. 4. In Sanity, create several `guide` and `announcement` documents and tag them for the 'coach' role. 5. Verify that this content is correctly displayed in the appropriate widgets on the dashboard. 6. Confirm that content tagged for other roles does not appear.

subtasks.title: Create 'announcement' Schema in Sanity Studio
subtasks.description: Define and implement a new document schema named `announcement` in the Sanity Studio codebase. This schema is essential for creating coach-specific announcements that will be displayed on the new dashboard.
subtasks.details: In the Sanity Studio project, create a new schema file (e.g., `schemas/announcement.js`). Define the schema with a `name` of 'announcement' and a `title` of 'Announcement'. It must include the following fields: `title` (type: string), `date` (type: date), `body` (type: blockContent), and `targetRole` (type: string). For the `targetRole` field, use a predefined list of options (e.g., ['coach', 'staff']) to ensure data consistency. Register the new schema in your Sanity configuration so it appears in the Studio.
subtasks.testStrategy: Deploy the Sanity Studio changes. Open the Studio and verify that 'Announcement' appears as a new content type. Create a new announcement, fill in all fields, set the target role to 'coach', and publish it to confirm the schema works correctly.

subtasks.title: Scaffold the Coach Dashboard Page and Route
subtasks.description: Create the basic file structure and routing for the new coach dashboard page. This involves creating a new page component at the designated route and ensuring it is protected by the general authentication middleware.
subtasks.details: In the Next.js application, create a new route at `/portal/dashboard/page.tsx`. Implement a basic React server component named `CoachDashboardPage` that returns a placeholder heading like "Coach Dashboard". Ensure this route is covered by the existing Clerk middleware for protected routes, so that only authenticated users can access it. At this stage, do not implement role-specific logic or data fetching.
subtasks.testStrategy: After implementing, run the application. As an unauthenticated user, try to navigate to `/portal/dashboard` and verify you are redirected to the sign-in page. Log in as any authenticated user and verify you can access the page and see the placeholder heading.

subtasks.title: Implement Coach Role-Specific Access Guard
subtasks.description: Enhance the coach dashboard page to restrict access exclusively to users with the 'coach' role. Users with other roles or without a defined role should be redirected away.
subtasks.details: In the `CoachDashboardPage` component (`/portal/dashboard/page.tsx`), implement a server-side check to fetch the current user's role from the Supabase `users` table. This logic should leverage the existing pattern from Task 7 to query Supabase using the authenticated user's ID from Clerk. If the user's role is not 'coach', use the `redirect` function from `next/navigation` to send them to a default authenticated page (e.g., `/portal`). If the role is 'coach', allow the page content to render.
subtasks.testStrategy: 1. Log in as a user with the 'coach' role and confirm you can access `/portal/dashboard`. 2. Log in as an authenticated user with a different role (e.g., 'staff' or a regular user) and verify you are redirected away from `/portal/dashboard` to the specified default page.

subtasks.title: Fetch Coach-Specific Content from Sanity
subtasks.description: Implement the data-fetching logic within the `CoachDashboardPage` component to query Sanity for content targeted specifically at coaches.
subtasks.details: In the `CoachDashboardPage` server component, write and execute two separate asynchronous GROQ queries using the Sanity client. The first query will fetch the latest `announcement` documents where `targetRole == 'coach'`, ordered by date descending. The second query will fetch `guide` documents where `targetRole == 'coach'`. Pass the fetched data as props from the page component to the client components that will render them.
subtasks.testStrategy: Before implementation, create at least two announcements and two guides in Sanity, setting `targetRole` to 'coach' for one of each and a different role for the others. In the `CoachDashboardPage` component, `console.log` the fetched data on the server side. Verify that only the documents with `targetRole == 'coach'` are returned from the queries.

subtasks.title: Implement Dashboard UI with Grid and Content Widgets
subtasks.description: Build the user interface for the coach dashboard, organizing the fetched content into a clear grid layout with distinct widgets for announcements and guides.
subtasks.details: Using the data fetched in the previous subtask, implement the UI for the `CoachDashboardPage`. Use a CSS grid or a component library's grid system (e.g., Tailwind CSS grid classes) to structure the layout. Create separate client components for the content widgets, such as `AnnouncementsWidget.tsx` and `GuidesWidget.tsx`. These components will receive the fetched data as props. The `AnnouncementsWidget` should display a list of announcement titles and dates. The `GuidesWidget` should display a list of featured guide titles. Style these components to be clean, readable, and consistent with the portal's existing design.
subtasks.testStrategy: Log in as a coach. Verify the dashboard page displays a grid layout. Confirm the 'Latest Announcements' and 'Featured Guides' widgets are present and correctly populated with the data fetched from Sanity. Check the layout's responsiveness on different screen sizes (desktop, tablet, mobile).
